title: keeping up with AI is making you fall behind (just focus on this one thing)
summary: Liam Ottley argues that keeping up with AI news creates anxiety and distraction. He recommends niching down rather than chasing every release, using pattern thinking to spot trends, and focusing on AI Operating Systems as a major market opportunity.
writeup: An AI agency builder makes the case that anxiety about the AI news cycle is a symptom of an unmade decision, not a knowledge deficit, citing Andrej Karpathy's public admission of feeling behind despite being at the frontier. The through-line is goal-first focus: once a practitioner knows whether they are building a $10K/month service business or something larger, the vast majority of daily AI news becomes irrelevant noise to filter out. The concrete prescription is to pick a narrow problem domain, wrap a Claude Code AI operating system around it, and exploit the transferability of that template across clients rather than chasing each new model release.
key_points: Andrej Karpathy, co-founder of OpenAI and originator of the term 'vibe coding', publicly stated he has never felt more behind as an engineer, illustrating that the anxiety is structural and affects even the most technically capable people in the field.

Recommendation and social-media algorithms are tuned to maximize emotional response, defaulting to fear-based and FOMO-based content, so deliberate algorithmic diet management (e.g., substituting history podcasts) is presented as a practical countermeasure, not a passive hope.
Technology from 2024 remains largely unimplemented inside real businesses, meaning the implementation gap is where the commercial opportunity sits, not in tracking the latest model releases.

The speaker frames the AI agency space around two models: transformation partner (audit, automate, integrate existing businesses) versus AI-first co-founder (build a new venture from scratch alongside an operator, with agents and automations as the default staffing layer).

Claude Code AI operating systems generate compounding returns from niche specialization because the context, templates, and integrations built for one client can be re-templated for the next with minimal marginal effort, making a niche agency far more scalable than a generalist one.

The 'unfair advantage' audit is the recommended niche-selection framework: the best niche is the intersection of prior domain knowledge, personal passion, and privileged access, not the hottest technology vertical.
